ideas.title: Adapting Counterfactual Analysis from Clinical Drug Trials to Evaluate Smart Specialization Policies
ideas.description: This idea applies an analogical solution from clinical pharmacology to the problem of policy evaluation. In drug development, when a randomized controlled trial is not feasible, researchers use propensity score matching to construct a credible counterfactual control group from observational data, enabling causal inference about a treatment's effect. We adapt this method to evaluate smart specialization policies. By treating policy implementation as the 'treatment,' we can use propensity score matching to identify a control group of non-participating regions that are statistically equivalent to the treated region(s) before the policy began. The subsequent divergence in key innovation and economic metrics between the treated group and its matched control provides a robust, quasi-experimental estimate of the policy's causal impact, overcoming the common limitations of simple before-after or cross-sectional comparisons.

ideas.title: Coalition-Actuated Policy Design: Engineering Instruments with Built-In Political Feasibility
ideas.description: This research develops a method for designing concrete policy instruments and governance structures that are pre-validated for political feasibility. It begins by mapping the ecosystem of stakeholders, veto players, and institutional rules relevant to a policy platform. Proposed instruments are then stress-tested against this map to identify coalitional gaps and points of resistance. The core output is a redesigned policy instrument bundle paired with a specific coalition-building and institutional navigation strategy, ensuring the design is actionable within real-world political constraints.

ideas.title: Designing Policy Action Lines as Modular, Interoperable Protocols
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution to building scalable, decentralized computer networks to the problem of designing concrete policy instruments. The analogous field is computer networking, where the Internet Protocol Suite (TCP/IP) solved interoperability through layered, modular protocols with clear functions and interfaces, governed by an open RFC process. The adapted idea is to design specific policy action lines not as monolithic plans, but as a stack of interoperable 'policy protocols.' This involves defining functional layers for a policy platform (e.g., a data layer for metrics, a redistribution layer for transfers, an application layer for services), specifying the concrete instruments and standards for each, and establishing a participatory governance structure—modeled on the RFC process—for developing, testing, and ratifying these instrument specifications to ensure they work together coherently in implementation.

ideas.title: Portfolio Immunization Metrics for Policy Strategy Risk-Reward
ideas.description: This research adapts the risk-benefit quantification framework from immunology and vaccinology to the domain of policy strategy analysis. In vaccinology, interventions are empirically evaluated through dual metrics: protective efficacy (measured in controlled trials as reduction in disease incidence) and real-world effectiveness, while adverse events are systematically monitored through surveillance systems. Adapted to policy diversification strategies, this approach would develop analogous metrics: 'Policy Protective Efficacy' to measure how effectively a strategy reduces vulnerability to specific risks or shocks, and 'Policy Strategy Effectiveness' to measure its real-world performance in achieving positive outcomes. A parallel 'Policy Adverse Event Surveillance' framework would systematically track unintended negative consequences. Together, these metrics would create an empirical dashboard for quantifying the complete risk-reward profile of different diversification strategies, enabling more rigorous comparison and optimization of policy approaches based on their demonstrated protective benefits and generative potential.
